region_of_origin: India (South Indian / coastal influence); served in Singapore
food_subgroup: Prawn / shrimp curry with coconut gravy

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists for Indian coconut prawn curry specifically; FOODON_03311737 (shrimp dish) used as closest parent.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP (Crustaceans, cooked) is the closest defensible parent; no specific code for Indian coconut prawn curry exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept found for this specific prepared dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One typical restaurant serving of Jhinga Nisha is approximately 250 g, comprising prawns in coconut-based curry gravy, usually served alongside rice or naan bread.

nutrient_source.title: Prawn curry (coconut-based) — estimated from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods and USDA FoodData Central shrimp and coconut milk entries
nutrient_source.organization: ICMR-NIN (Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition); USDA FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Jhinga Nisha is a chef's special dish not individually listed in any approved nutrient database. Values are estimated by combining ICMR-NIN data for cooked prawn curry (coconut-based) with USDA FoodData Central reference values for cooked shrimp (FDC ID 175180) and coconut milk (FDC ID 169411). Proportions estimated as approximately 45% prawn, 40% coconut-based gravy, 15% other ingredients. Per-100g values represent the combined dish.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of mixed seafood curry dishes — analogous estimate based on prawn/shrimp curry with coconut milk; reference: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Jhinga Nisha. GI estimated at 25 based on analogous low-carbohydrate seafood curry dishes with coconut milk. Coconut milk-based curries with minimal starch content consistently show low GI values (typically 20–35) in the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature. Glycemic load per 250 g serving calculated as (25 × 3.2 × 2.5) / 100 = 2.0.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~320 mg per 100 g; ~800 mg per serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to reduce sodium intake, as a 250 g serving contributes approximately 800 mg sodium — roughly one-third of the recommended daily limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~25) and low carbohydrate content (~3.2 g per 100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Jhinga Nisha has a low glycemic index and low carbohydrate content, resulting in a glycemic load of approximately 2 per serving, making it a suitable protein-rich option for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes when served without high-GI accompaniments such as white rice.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from coconut milk (~5.1 g per 100 g) and dietary cholesterol from prawns (~115 mg per 100 g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The coconut milk base contributes significant saturated fat (medium-chain triglycerides), and prawns contribute dietary cholesterol; individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume this dish in moderation and prefer smaller portions alongside high-fibre vegetables.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Jhinga Nisha is an Indian chef's special prawn curry dish in which fresh prawns (jhinga) are simmered in a rich, aromatic coconut-based gravy. The dish originates from the Indian coastal culinary tradition where coconut milk is used extensively to create creamy, mildly spiced curries. The gravy typically includes coconut milk, onion, tomato, ginger, garlic, and a blend of Indian spices such as turmeric, cumin, coriander, and chilli. The prawns are cooked until tender and absorb the flavours of the coconut gravy. The texture is creamy and slightly thick, with a mild to medium spice level. The flavour profile is rich, slightly sweet from the coconut milk, with savoury and aromatic spice notes. In Singapore, this dish is served at Indian restaurants such as Indian House / Dumpukht Grills & Curry at Boat Quay, typically accompanied by steamed basmati rice, naan, or roti. Nutritionally, Jhinga Nisha is a high-protein, moderate-fat dish with low carbohydrate content. Per 100 g it provides approximately 118 kcal, 11.5 g protein, 7.2 g fat (predominantly saturated fat from coconut milk), and 3.2 g carbohydrate. The dish is naturally gluten-free and dairy-free. It has a low glycemic index (~25) making it suitable for blood glucose management. The sodium content (~320 mg per 100 g) warrants attention for individuals with hypertension. The saturated fat from coconut milk and cholesterol from prawns require moderation for those with cardiovascular disease. Prawns are a good source of vitamin B12, selenium, phosphorus, and zinc.
common_user_queries: Is Jhinga Nisha good for diabetics?
What is in coconut prawn curry?

confidence_reason: Jhinga Nisha is a chef's special dish not individually listed in any approved nutrient database (ICMR-NIN, USDA, HPB). Nutrient values are estimated by combining ICMR-NIN prawn curry data with USDA FoodData Central reference values for cooked shrimp and coconut milk. GI is an analogous estimate from peer-reviewed literature. Confidence is medium rather than low because the ingredient composition is well-described and the estimation methodology is defensible.
notes: Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the primary source databases for this dish type. Trans fat is reported as 0.0 (confirmed zero — coconut milk and prawn-based dishes contain negligible industrially produced trans fats; naturally occurring trans fats from marine sources are below detection threshold in the reference sources used). All other -1 values represent nutrients not individually reported in the estimation sources; they are not confirmed zero. Nutrient values are estimated from ICMR-NIN and USDA FoodData Central component data and should be verified against laboratory analysis if used for clinical or regulatory purposes. GI of 25 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Jhinga Nisha was found in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature as of the accessed date. Serving size of 250 g is an estimate for a typical restaurant portion. Price data (SGD 18.38) preserved from field survey data source record for reference only; not used as a nutritional evidence source.
